ucs.title: [LLM] APT19 (Codoso) Office macro spawning hidden/encoded PowerShell — 2017 law-firm phishing chain
ucs.description: Hunts the signature APT19/Codoso 2017 spearphishing chain where a weaponized RTF/XLSM dropper from Outlook spawns Excel/Word, which in turn launches powershell.exe with a hidden window and base64-encoded payload to fetch the Codoso second stage. This combination of Office-parent → hidden+encoded PowerShell is the exact behavioral fingerprint FireEye documented for the May 2017 campaign against seven US law and investment firms.
ucs.splunk:
| tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.parent_process) as parent_cmdline from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name IN ("excel.exe","winword.exe","outlook.exe","eqnedt32.exe") AND Processes.process_name IN ("powershell.exe","powershell_ise.exe","pwsh.exe") AND (Processes.process="*-w*hidden*" OR Processes.process="*-windowstyle*hidden*" OR Processes.process="*-w*1*") AND (Processes.process="*-enc*" OR Processes.process="*-e *" OR Processes.process="*-encodedcommand*" OR Processes.process="*FromBase64String*" OR Processes.process="*IEX*" OR Processes.process="*DownloadString*") by host, user, Processes.parent_process_name, Processes.process_name dest=host
| `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)`
| where len(cmdline) > 200 OR match(cmdline, "(?i)(http|https)://")
| `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ("excel.exe","winword.exe","outlook.exe","eqnedt32.exe")
| where FileName in~ ("powershell.exe","powershell_ise.exe","pwsh.exe")
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("-w hidden","-WindowStyle Hidden","-windowstyle 1","-w 1")
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("-enc","-EncodedCommand","FromBase64String","IEX","DownloadString","Net.WebClient")
| extend cmdLen = strlen(ProcessCommandLine)
| where cmdLen > 200 or ProcessCommandLine matches regex @"(?i)https?://"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, FolderPath, SHA256
| join kind=leftouter (DeviceNetworkEvents | where Timestamp > ago(30d) | where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "powershell.exe" | project DeviceName, RemoteUrl, RemoteIP, NetTime=Timestamp) on DeviceName
| where NetTime between (Timestamp .. Timestamp + 5m) or isempty(NetTime)
ucs.rationale: APT19/Codoso's 2017 law-firm campaign (FireEye reporting, May 2017) used XLSM attachments invoking VBA → hidden, base64-encoded PowerShell pulling the Codoso/Sunshop downloader. The very tight tuple of Outlook/Office parent → powershell.exe with both hidden-window AND encoded-command flags AND outbound HTTP within seconds is rare in benign telemetry and is exactly the chain APT19 reused across its

ucs.title: [LLM] APT19 Squiblydoo — regsvr32 /s /n /u /i: scrobj.dll fetching remote SCT (AppLocker bypass)
ucs.description: Hunts APT19's documented use of regsvr32.exe with a remote .sct/scrobj.dll payload (the 'Squiblydoo' AppLocker/whitelist bypass) that the group adopted alongside their 2017 phishing chain to stage Codoso implants without dropping a PE to disk. Detects the precise command-line shape regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:<URL or UNC> scrobj.dll.
ucs.splunk:
| tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.parent_process_name) as parent from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_name="regsvr32.exe" AND Processes.process="*scrobj.dll*" AND Processes.process="*/i:*" AND (Processes.process="*http*" OR Processes.process="*\\\\*" OR Processes.process="*.sct*") by host, user, Processes.process_name, Processes.parent_process_name dest=host
| `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)`
| join type=left host [| tstats summariesonly=true values(All_Traffic.dest) as remote_dest values(All_Traffic.dest_port) as ports from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic where All_Traffic.app="regsvr32.exe" by All_Traffic.src | rename All_Traffic.src as host]
| `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where FileName =~ "regsvr32.exe"
| where ProcessCommandLine has "scrobj.dll"
| where ProcessCommandLine matches regex @"(?i)/i:\s*(https?://|\\\\|file://)"
   or ProcessCommandLine has ".sct"
| extend SilentFlags = ProcessCommandLine has_all ("/s","/n","/u","/i:")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, SilentFlags, SHA256
| join kind=leftouter (
    DeviceNetworkEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(30d)
    | where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "regsvr32.exe"
    | project DeviceName, RemoteUrl, RemoteIP, RemotePort, NetTime=Timestamp
) on DeviceName
| where NetTime between (Timestamp .. Timestamp + 2m) or isempty(NetTime)
ucs.rationale: Squiblydoo (regsvr32 /s /n /u /i:<URL> scrobj.dll) is a hallmark APT19/Codoso AppLocker-bypass technique called out in MITRE ATT&CK G0073 mappings to T1218.010 and in FireEye/Carbon Black reporting on the 2017 law-firm intrusions. regsvr32.exe legitimately initiating outbound HTTP to fetch a remote scriptlet is essentially never benign in enterprise environments, giving very low FP versus broader
